Q1: I tried to keep a somewhat consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings. Being a visual learner, I'd spend time creating diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture materials. With about 20 hours a week dedicated to studying, I had to be strategic since I don't have a lot of extra resources to fall back on. I'd often batch my study sessions around when I knew I could focus best, though honestly some weeks were harder than others to stick to the plan.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to have regular times blocked out, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're working with limited resources and juggling responsibilities. Deadlines would sometimes force me into longer sessions than I'd planned. I found that the weeks leading up to assignments were much more intense than the quieter periods between them.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new content posted or when an assignment deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd just check in to see if there were discussion posts I should respond to since I tried to stay active in those. The notification emails would prompt me too, reminding me there was something new to look at. I logged in pretty frequently overall, probably out of habit more than anything.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became kind of automatic for me, especially in the mornings with my coffee. I'd scroll through announcements and see what videos were posted. After a few weeks, it just felt natural to open it up like checking email. The discussions helped make it feel more like a community space I wanted to return to rather than just a content dump.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most valuable for me since I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I'd watch them multiple times sometimes, pausing to sketch out concepts in my notes. The discussion forums were also really helpful because hearing other students' perspectives helped solidify my understanding. When peers would share their interpretations of complex topics, it often clicked for me in ways the lectures alone didn't achieve.
Q6: Some of the text-heavy readings I'd skim through pretty quickly or delay until I absolutely needed them. Without strong visuals or diagrams, dense reading materials just didn't stick as well for me. I also didn't use some of the supplementary resources that seemed redundant to what was covered in videos. When you're pressed for time and resources, you have to prioritize what actually helps you learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams, or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content like diagrams, flowcharts, or well-structured video explanations always felt valuable. If I walked away feeling like I actually learned something I could apply, then it was time well spent. The peer discussions often felt worthwhile too because they made me think differently about the material.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. With limited time and resources, I had to focus on what would directly impact my grades. Required activities had urgency built in, so I'd prioritize those. But when I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I was genuinely curious or needed extra help understanding something, which made it feel more personal and less like a chore.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I could, then study from them offline to save on data and because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd create handwritten visual notes and diagrams that I'd review repeatedly away from my computer. I also used some free YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't fully grasp from the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed notes spread across my desk.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my hand-drawn concept maps or flipping through printed slides with no LMS activity showing. I'd spend hours reorganizing my visual notes, connecting ideas with arrows and colors, which helped me process information deeply. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, talking through concepts together. None of that would show up in any system, but it was some of my most productive learning time.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and then work with those materials offline for days. My internet can be spotty and data costs add up, so downloading and working offline just made practical sense. I'd revisit those downloaded materials multiple times before an exam without ever logging back in to access them again.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became harder, usually when I was feeling down or sad about things in my personal life. Sometimes the weight of financial stress or just general life difficulties made it hard to focus on coursework. My attendance dropped during those times even though I tried to keep up with assignments. The CBT sessions I attended helped me work through some of those low periods and get back on track.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was overwhelmed with work or personal stuff, I'd log in less frequently and just grab what I needed quickly. Fatigue was real too—after long days, watching lecture videos felt exhausting even though I knew I should. I'd sometimes just download materials to review later when I had more mental energy, which meant my online activity looked minimal even when I was planning to study.
Q14: There were times I'd be watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere, just going through the motions. I might scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. When I was feeling particularly sad or distracted, I'd access materials but not retain much of anything. It's frustrating because you're putting in the time but not getting the benefit.
Q15: I definitely postponed some readings and optional activities, knowing they were important but feeling unable to tackle them in the moment. Sometimes the sadness I was carrying made it hard to motivate myself even for things I knew mattered. I'd tell myself I'd get to it tomorrow, and sometimes tomorrow would stretch into several days. The assignments I couldn't postpone because of deadlines, but everything else was fair game for procrastination during harder periods.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my biggest motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly I'd be logged in constantly, watching videos, checking rubrics, participating in discussions. My activity would spike significantly in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I think I would have spread my engagement out more evenly, but the structure they provided kept me accountable even when my motivation was low.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my engagement. When I got positive feedback on an assignment, it boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more with similar activities. Constructive criticism would send me back to the materials to understand where I went wrong. I appreciated feedback that was specific because it helped me know exactly what to focus on. It made the whole process feel more like a dialogue than just submitting work into a void.
Q18: The course structure helped keep me on track even when my motivation dipped. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap to follow. The way content was organized visually in the LMS made it easier for me to navigate and plan my studying. I appreciated when the structure was predictable because it reduced the mental energy needed to figure out what I should be doing next.
Q19: The visual organization of the course made engagement easier for me—clear headings, well-labeled videos, logical progression through topics. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy readings without visual aids. Also, when discussion prompts were vague, I'd struggle to know what kind of contribution was expected. Having limited resources meant I needed the course design to be efficient and not require a lot of extra materials I couldn't access.
Q20: I think students might be searching for something, maybe struggling to understand content and clicking around trying to find clarity. They could also be anxious about the course and checking things repeatedly without feeling ready to actually produce work. Sometimes high activity might mean someone is engaged but facing barriers to completing assignments—maybe personal issues, confusion about requirements, or perfectionism holding them back from submitting.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. I'd access materials, download them, and then do a lot of my studying offline where it wouldn't show up in the LMS. My exam scores and assignment grades were decent because I was putting in work, just not always in ways the system could track. Students who learn well independently or who use external resources might look less active online while still performing well academically.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on videos. That's useful for seeing general trends. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional states affecting learning, or the depth of understanding someone develops. The numbers tell part of the story but not the whole picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections, asking questions in my head or in discussions. For me, engagement looks like creating those visual notes, discussing ideas with peers, and feeling genuinely curious about the content. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams at my kitchen table. It wouldn't capture the conversations with classmates outside the platform or the YouTube videos I watched to clarify concepts. It definitely wouldn't show the emotional journey—the sad days when I pushed through anyway, or how the CBT sessions helped me stay resilient. My engagement was real and consistent, even when my online footprint didn't fully reflect it.
